title: Month-End Close & Financial Statements
subhead: The five-day close calendar that turns a month of transactions into auditable financial statements, the adjusting entries that make accruals honest, and the three statements that roll up the result.
hook: "Walk me through a month-end close." The interview answer is the five-day timeline, the four types of adjusting entries, and the $242,000 January net income that comes out the other side. This module is that entire walkthrough.

chunks.title: Recall check — Modules 01–04 (spaced repetition)
chunks.intro: Before we start Month-End Close, let's make sure AR, billing, and the earlier modules are locked in.

questions.q: Under ASC 606, which of the 5 steps triggers revenue recognition?
options: Recognize revenue when/as each obligation is satisfied
questions.explanation: Revenue is recognized at step 5 — when (or as) each distinct performance obligation is satisfied. The contract and price steps set it up; satisfaction books it.

questions.q: A client prepays $120,000 before work begins. What is credited?
questions.explanation: Cash received before the obligation is satisfied is a liability — Deferred Revenue. It becomes revenue only when the work is delivered.

questions.q: Skarion writes off TeleCom Builders' $112,700 balance. What is the journal entry?
options: Dr Bad Debt Expense / Cr AR
Dr Allowance for Doubtful Accounts / Cr Accounts Receivable
Dr Allowance / Cr Bad Debt Expense
questions.explanation: Write-off goes against the existing allowance: Dr Allowance / Cr AR. Bad Debt Expense was already recorded when the allowance was established — it is not touched at write-off.

questions.q: What is Days Sales Outstanding (DSO) and how is it calculated?
questions.explanation: DSO = Average AR ÷ Credit Revenue × Days. Skarion: $997,700 ÷ $4.8M × 365 ≈ 75.8 days. Lower is better; controllers watch it month over month.

questions.q: What is the difference between Unbilled AR (WIP) and regular AR?
options: WIP is work performed but not yet billable; AR is invoiced and awaiting payment
WIP is a liability; AR is an asset
Regular AR relates to completed contracts only
questions.explanation: WIP/Unbilled AR captures value created before a billing milestone triggers (percentage-of-completion). Regular AR is an invoice already cut and awaiting cash.

questions.q: What is the allowance for doubtful accounts and why is it required by GAAP?
options: A cash reserve set aside for bad customers
A contra-asset estimating uncollectible AR in the period of sale (matching principle)
An expense recorded only when an invoice defaults
questions.explanation: The allowance is a contra-asset that reduces net AR. GAAP requires estimating uncollectible AR in the period the sale is made — the matching principle.

questions.q: A customer pays $190,000 of a $405,000 balance and disputes the rest. What do you record?
options: Dr Cash $190,000 / Cr AR $190,000; leave $215,000 open with a dispute flag (no write-off)
Hold the $190,000 in a suspense account until the dispute is resolved
questions.explanation: Apply the cash to the oldest invoice and document the dispute on the remainder — never silently write off a disputed balance. The remaining $215,000 stays open in the subledger.

questions.q: What is the two-sided bank reconciliation method?
options: Match the bank to the GL line by line and post the difference
Adjusted bank balance = statement + deposits in transit − outstanding checks must equal adjusted book balance = GL + credits − debits not recorded
Force the GL to equal the bank statement
questions.explanation: Both sides adjust for timing, then must meet exactly. Adjusted bank = statement + deposits in transit − outstanding checks; adjusted book = GL + unrecorded credits − unrecorded debits. Equal, or there's a real error.

chunks.title: What Happens During Month-End Close — A Real Close, Step by Step
chunks.intro: Month-end close is when accounting stops being data entry and becomes a high-stakes deadline operation. Everything in the right order, on the right day, nothing skipped.
chunks.transcript:
0:00 — Month-end close is when accounting stops being an ongoing data-entry job and becomes a high-stakes deadline operation. Everything has to be done in the right order, on the right day, and nothing can be skipped.

0:30 — Here is Skarion's five-day close schedule. Day one: lock all timesheets and AP — any invoice not entered by 5 PM goes into next month. Day two: post payroll, employer taxes, and all offshore contractor accruals. Day three: calculate WIP, post depreciation, post the loan interest accrual. Day four: bank reconciliation, tie the AR subledger to the GL, tie the AP subledger to the GL. Day five: review the P&L against budget, produce the financial package, get controller sign-off.

2:00 — The hardest part for a services firm is the adjusting entries — entries for things that happened but haven't been invoiced yet. Your offshore drafting team worked 400 hours in January. The invoice won't arrive until February 4th. But the expense belongs in January. So on January 31st, you accrue it: debit COGS Subcontractors $14,800, credit Accrued Liabilities $14,800.

3:00 — Then on February 1st, you reverse that accrual. When the invoice actually arrives on the 4th, you post it normally. The reversal ensures the expense doesn't get double-counted.

4:00 — The close playbook in your course materials lists every entry you need to post during Skarion's January close. Work through it in SkarionBooks — the right order, the right amounts, every one.

chunks.title: The month-end close process
chunks.bodyHtml:
Month-end close exists because financial statements are produced once per period, and they must be accurate, complete, and timely. Incomplete or wrong statements lead to bad decisions, missed covenants, and audit findings. The close is governed by a close checklist in which every task has an owner, a due date, a status, and supporting documentation.

The discipline that makes a close work: lock the prior period once it is closed so late entries can't silently change history, and tie every subledger (AR, AP, payroll) back to the GL control account before you declare the period done.

callout.html: Hard close vs. soft close. A hard close locks the period permanently — no further entries except through a formal adjustment in the next period. A soft close leaves the period open for corrections up to the audit, usually for recurring accrual estimates. Public companies essentially always hard-close; private mid-size firms like Skarion typically soft-close then hard-close at year-end.

chunks.title: Skarion's 5-day close timeline
chunks.bodyHtml:
This is the actual five-day calendar Skarion runs to close January. Memorize the rhythm — it is what most mid-size services firms look like:

DayDateFocus
Day 1Feb 1Lock timesheets, lock AP, generate AP aging, post all outstanding invoices.
Day 2Feb 2Post payroll entry and employer taxes, book all offshore contractor accruals, amortize prepaids.
Day 3Feb 3Calculate WIP, post depreciation, post loan interest accrual, run trial balance.
Day 4Feb 4Bank reconciliation, tie AR subledger to GL, tie AP subledger to GL.
Day 5Feb 5Review P&L vs. prior month and budget, produce financial statement package, controller sign-off.

Notice the order: cut off the inputs (timesheets, AP, invoices) first, post the accruals and adjustments second, prove the balances tie third, then produce and review statements. You cannot build statements on a trial balance that hasn't been tied.

steps.title: Day 1 — Lock & post
steps.desc: Lock timesheets and AP. Generate the AP aging. Post every outstanding invoice so the period's revenue and liabilities are complete.

steps.title: Day 2 — People cost
steps.desc: Post the payroll entry and employer taxes. Accrue offshore contractor work done but not yet invoiced. Amortize prepaids for the month.

steps.title: Day 3 — Adjustments
steps.desc: Calculate WIP revenue. Post depreciation. Accrue loan interest. Run the unadjusted trial balance.

steps.title: Day 4 — Tie & reconcile
steps.desc: Bank reconciliation. Tie AR subledger total to GL account 1100. Tie AP subledger total to GL account 2000.

steps.title: Day 5 — Report & sign-off
steps.desc: Review P&L vs prior month and budget. Produce the financial statement package. Controller signs off and the period is closed.

chunks.title: Adjusting entries — and the four types
chunks.bodyHtml:
Adjusting entries exist because the accrual principle requires recording things that have happened but generated no paperwork yet. They are why the close takes five days instead of one. There are exactly four types:

TypeSkarion example
Accrued RevenuesEngineers spent 400 hours on an HLD that is 60% complete and not yet billable — book Unbilled AR (WIP) and revenue.
Accrued ExpensesOffshore drafting done Jan 31, invoice not arriving until Feb 4 — accrue COGS now.
Deferred RevenuesClient prepaid $120,000; deliver the work and release it from Deferred Revenue to earned revenue.
Deferred Expenses$36,000 prepaid Vetro license amortized $3,000/month to Software Expense.

chunks.title: Accrue offshore drafting done but not yet invoiced
chunks.scenarioHtml: Global CAD Solutions finished $14,800 of drafting on January 31st. The invoice arrives February 4th. Under accrual accounting the expense belongs in January. Accrue it now (reverse it on Feb 1 before the real invoice is entered).

chunks.title: Amortize the Vetro license for January
chunks.scenarioHtml: The $36,000 Vetro FiberMap license was paid July 1st and recorded as Prepaid Software. 12-month straight-line amortization = $3,000/month. Record January's amortization: move $3,000 from the Prepaid asset into Software Expense.

chunks.title: Post monthly straight-line depreciation
chunks.scenarioHtml: Skarion capitalizes 5 GIS workstations at $12,000 each ($60,000 total), 5-year useful life, $0 salvage. Straight-line depreciation = $1,000/month total ($200/month each). Record January's depreciation.

chunks.title: Accrue January loan interest
chunks.scenarioHtml: Skarion carries a $500,000 term loan at 6.5% annual interest. January's accrued interest = $500,000 × 6.5% ÷ 12 = $2,708.33. Accrue the interest (it has been incurred even though the cash payment is later).

chunks.title: Recognize unbilled WIP revenue
chunks.scenarioHtml: Engineers spent 400 hours on an HLD in January at a blended rate of $85/hr = $34,000 of work. The HLD is 60% complete and not yet billable. Under the percentage-of-completion method, recognize the earned-but-unbilled portion: Dr Unbilled AR (WIP) $34,000 / Cr Engineering Design Revenue $34,000.

chunks.title: The three core financial statements
chunks.bodyHtml:
Once adjustments are posted and the adjusted trial balance balances, it rolls up into the three statements management actually reads:

1. Income Statement — Revenue − COGS − Operating Expenses = Net Income. Skarion January: $480,000 revenue − $140,000 COGS − $98,000 OpEx = $242,000 Net Income. This is performance over a period.

2. Balance Sheet — Assets = Liabilities + Equity. A snapshot of financial position at a point in time (e.g. January 31). Every adjusting entry from the close flows here: prepaid amortization, depreciation, accruals, WIP.

3. Cash Flow Statement — three sections: Operating (cash from running the business), Investing (capex, asset purchases), and Financing (loans, equity, distributions). Critically: a profitable business can run out of cash if AR isn't collected. The cash flow statement is where that gap becomes visible.

Key ratios at month-end:

RatioFormulaSkarion Jan
Gross margin %(Revenue − COGS) ÷ Revenue($480K − $140K) ÷ $480K = 70.8%
Operating margin %(Revenue − COGS − OpEx) ÷ Revenue$242K ÷ $480K = 50.4%
Current ratioCurrent Assets ÷ Current Liabilitiescash & AR heavy — liquidity check
DSOAverage AR ÷ Credit Revenue × 365$997,700 ÷ $4.8M × 365 = 75.8 days

chunks.intro: Four close and financial-statement questions, the exact ones that come up in entry-level interviews.
chunks.title: Month-end close & financial statements quiz

questions.q: What is the purpose of a closing entry at year-end?
options: To close temporary (Revenue/Expense) accounts to Retained Earnings so the new year starts clean
To delete all transactions from the period
questions.explanation: Closing entries reset temporary accounts (Revenue and Expense) to zero by transferring their balances to Retained Earnings. Permanent accounts carry forward; the next period starts with empty revenue/expense accounts.

questions.q: Skarion's January revenue is $480,000. COGS is $140,000. What is the gross profit margin?
questions.explanation: Gross profit = $480,000 − $140,000 = $340,000. Margin = $340,000 ÷ $480,000 = 70.8%. (50.4% would be operating margin after also subtracting $98,000 OpEx.)

questions.q: Which financial statement shows whether the company can pay its bills next month (liquidity)?
questions.explanation: Liquidity is a snapshot question — answered by the Balance Sheet, specifically the Current Assets vs Current Liabilities comparison (the Current Ratio). The income statement is performance over time; the cash flow statement is cash movement.

questions.q: Skarion is profitable but running low on cash. Which statement reveals why?
questions.explanation: The Cash Flow Statement reconciles profit to cash. If cash is falling despite profit, the answer is almost always in Operations — usually uncollected AR (or growing inventory/prepaids). The Cash Flow Statement makes the gap visible.

chunks.title: Scenario Lab — The Month Where Everything Went Wrong
chunks.intro: Skarion's February close has seven compounding errors seeded in the data. Find and fix all seven before the trial balance balances.
chunks.setup:
Download the February data workbook below. It contains an Instructions tab, a Raw Data tab (the unadjusted February trial balance + transactions), a Candidate Trial Balance tab, and a Corrections Log tab. Seven problems are baked in:

  1. Payroll understated — February gross was $82,450 (overtime month) but was posted at $79,167. Understated by $3,283.
  2. Vetro prepaid amortization skipped — $3,000 of monthly Software Expense and the corresponding reduction in Prepaid Software never recorded.
  3. January offshore accrual never reversed — the $14,800 January accrual was not reversed on Feb 1, so it still sits on the books.
  4. Cash posted to the wrong customer — a $215,000 customer wire was credited to Southeast Broadband instead of Atlantic Fiber Co.
  5. Depreciation typo — posted at $1,200 instead of the correct $1,000 (a $200 over). Less wrong-direction; still a correction.
  6. Bank fee unrecorded — the $45 February monthly bank service charge never hit the books.
  7. Prepaid insurance forgotten — a policy paid in January ($1,800/quarter = $600/month) has never been amortized.
tasks: Open the workbook and identify each of the seven problems by tracing Raw Data against expectations.
Post each correction in the Corrections Log tab with: Problem, Journal Entry, and Dollar Impact.
Recompute the corrected February trial balance — total debits must equal total credits.
Write a one-page close memo for the controller summarizing the seven fixes and any that needed supervisor escalation (the cash-to-wrong-customer error requires it).
chunks.submissionPrompt: Paste your seven correcting journal entries (with amounts) and your corrected trial balance totals (total debits = total credits). Note any entry that required supervisor escalation.
chunks.solution:
Corrections:

  1. Dr 6000 Salaries — US Engineering PMs $3,283 / Cr 2200 Payroll Tax Liabilities (recompute the employer-tax side proportionally) — or simplest: Dr Salaries $3,283 / Cr 2200 Payroll Tax Liabilities $260 & Cr 1000 Checking $3,023 for the extra net+tax. Document the overtime.
  2. Dr 6500 Software & Subscriptions $3,000 / Cr 1400 Prepaid Software Licenses $3,000 — the skipped amortization.
  3. Dr 2400 Accrued Liabilities $14,800 / Cr 5100 COGS — Subcontractor Drafting $14,800 — reverse the January accrual so the real invoice (entered normally in February) doesn't double-count.
  4. Dr 1100 Accounts Receivable — Southeast Broadband $215,000 / Cr 1100 Accounts Receivable — Atlantic Fiber Co. $215,000 — net-zero to total AR, but corrects whose subledger it belongs in. Escalate to supervisor for sign-off.
  5. Dr 1590 Accumulated Depreciation $200 / Cr 6700 Depreciation Expense $200 — back out the $200 over.
  6. Dr 6900 Bank Fees Expense $45 / Cr 1000 Operating Checking $45 — book the unrecorded fee.
  7. Dr 6500 Software & Subscriptions (or dedicated Insurance Expense) $600 / Cr 1400 Prepaid (or Prepaid Insurance asset) $600 — record February's amortization of the prepaid insurance.

Net effect: each entry is balanced, total debits equal total credits, and the corrected February trial balance ties. The cash-to-wrong-customer and the un-reversed accrual are the two that needed documented escalation.

chunks.title: Scenario Lab — Fixed Assets: Capitalize or Expense?
chunks.intro: Five Q1 purchases — classify each as capital expenditure or operating expense, justify the call using the capitalization threshold, post the entries, and build the depreciation schedule.
chunks.setup:
Skarion makes five purchases in Q1. For each, decide capitalize (asset + depreciate) vs. expense immediately. The IRS de minimis safe harbor threshold is $2,500 per item (or $5,000 with audited financials). Apply judgment using both threshold and useful life.

PurchaseAmountNotes
5 new GIS workstations$14,500 each5-year useful life, $500 salvage each
Annual ArcGIS Pro license renewal$8,00012-month benefit
Desk chairs for engineering office$3,200Single cost; office furniture
Server rack for GIS database hosting$22,0007-year useful life, $0 salvage
Repair of existing server (fan replacement)$450Restores function, no improvement
tasks: Classify each purchase as capitalize or expense, and justify using the de minimis threshold and the useful-life / betterment tests.
Post the journal entry for each purchase.
Build the first-year depreciation schedule for each capitalized asset (straight-line; for assets bought mid-period, apply half-month convention or full-month per the policy you choose — state it).
Explain the one purchase that is a repair, not a betterment, and why it is expensed regardless of dollar amount.
chunks.submissionPrompt: Paste your five classification calls with justifications, the five journal entries, and the first-year depreciation schedule with monthly and annual amounts.
chunks.solution:
1. 5 GIS workstations @ $14,500 each = $72,500: Capitalize. Well above $2,500, 5-year useful life. Entry: Dr 1500 GIS Servers & Workstations $72,500 / Cr 1000 Operating Checking $72,500. Depreciation (straight-line, $500 salvage each → $14,000 depreciable × 5 = $70,000 / 5 yrs / 12 = $1,166.67/month total; first year ~$14,000).

2. Annual ArcGIS Pro $8,000: Capitalize as prepaid (intangible/subscription asset), not depreciate. Entry: Dr 1400 Prepaid Software Licenses $8,000 / Cr 1000 Operating Checking $8,000. Amortize $666.67/month ($8,000/12) to 6500 Software Expense over 12 months.

3. Desk chairs $3,200: This is the judgment call. Above the $2,500 de minimis safe harbor, so technically must be capitalized — BUT many firms have a higher internally-set capitalization threshold (often $5,000) for furniture. The defensible answer: capitalize under the strict IRS safe harbor, depreciate over ~5 years; or expense under an audit-supported $5,000 internal threshold if documented. State the policy you'd follow.

4. Server rack $22,000: Capitalize. 7-year life, $0 salvage. Entry: Dr 1500 GIS Servers & Workstations $22,000 / Cr 1000 $22,000. Depreciation $22,000 / 7 / 12 = $261.90/month; first year ~$3,143.

5. Server fan repair $450: Expense, regardless of amount. A repair restores existing function without improving the asset — it is never capitalized under the betterment test. Below the safe harbor too. Entry: Dr 6500 Software & Subscriptions (or Repairs & Maintenance) $450 / Cr 1000 $450.

chunks.title: Scenario Lab — Year-End and the Closing Entries
chunks.intro: Year-end: post the closing entries that zero out every temporary account and roll net income into Retained Earnings, then verify the post-closing trial balance.
chunks.setup: You are given Skarion's December 31 adjusted trial balance. All monthly closes were done correctly. Now post the closing entries to zero out every temporary account and roll net income into Retained Earnings.
tasks: Identify every temporary account (all revenue and expense accounts) on the December 31 trial balance.
Calculate Skarion's net income for the year (total revenue − total expenses).

Post the four closing entries: (a) close Revenue accounts to Income Summary; (b) close Expense accounts to Income Summary; (c) close Income Summary to Retained Earnings; (d) close any Drawings/Distributions to Retained Earnings if applicable.
Verify the post-closing trial balance — only permanent accounts (Assets, Liabilities, Equity) remain with balances.
Confirm Assets = Liabilities + Updated Equity after the close.
chunks.submissionPrompt: Paste the four closing entries with account names and amounts, your net income figure, and confirm Assets = Liabilities + Equity post-close (paste the totals).
chunks.solution:
Net income for the year ≈ annual revenue − annual COGS − annual OpEx (per the monthly pattern, ~$242,000/month net in the Jan example scaled across the year ≈ material annual figure — use the actual Dec 31 trial balance totals).

Closing entries:

  1. Close Revenue to Income Summary: Dr 4000 Engineering Design Revenue [annual total] / Dr 4100 Permitting Services Revenue [annual total] / Cr Income Summary [sum].
  2. Close Expenses to Income Summary: Dr Income Summary [sum of all expense accounts: 5100, 5200, 6000, 6100, 6400, 6500, 6700, 6800, 6900, 6720] / Cr each expense account [its annual total].
  3. Close Income Summary to Retained Earnings: If net income is positive (credits exceeded debits on Income Summary): Dr Income Summary [net income] / Cr 3100 Retained Earnings [net income]. If a loss, reverse.
  4. Close Drawings/Distributions (if the owner took a distribution): Dr 3000 Owner's Equity (or 3100 Retained Earnings) [distribution] / Cr 1000 Operating Checking [distribution].

Post-closing verification: only 1000s/1100s/1200s/1400/1500/1590/2000s/3000s remain with balances; all 4000s and 5000s–6999 are zero. Total assets = total liabilities + updated equity — still balances to the cent.

chunks.title: Scenario Lab — Build the Financial Statement Package
chunks.intro: Using the January adjusted trial balance, build the three core financial statements from scratch in Excel and write a 3-sentence CFO memo.
chunks.setup:
Use the January adjusted trial balance from Chunk 23 (the month-end close). Build the three core financial statements in Excel using the financial statement package template below:

  1. Income Statement: Revenue → COGS → Gross Profit → Operating Expenses → Net Income. Calculate Gross Margin % and Operating Margin %.
  2. Balance Sheet: Current Assets, Long-Term Assets, Current Liabilities, Long-Term Liabilities, Equity. Verify Assets = Liabilities + Equity.
  3. Cash Flow Statement (simplified indirect method): Start with Net Income, adjust for non-cash items (depreciation), change in working capital (AR, AP), investing activities (any capex), financing activities (loan repayment / distributions).
tasks: Download the financial statement package template and the January adjusted trial balance figures (from the month-end close module).
Build the Income Statement with sections and the two margin ratios (target: Gross Margin ~70.8%, Operating Margin ~50.4% for January per Chunk 23).
Build the Balance Sheet and confirm Assets = Liabilities + Equity on the bottom line.
Build the simplified indirect Cash Flow Statement and reconcile the ending cash to the bank rec's adjusted balance ($336,250).
Write a 3-sentence CFO memo: what happened financially in January, what concerns you, and what to watch in February.
chunks.submissionPrompt: Paste your three statement bottom lines (Net Income, Total Assets = Total Liabilities + Equity, ending Cash), your two margin ratios, your Cash Flow reconciliation to $336,250 ending cash, and your 3-sentence CFO memo.
chunks.solution:
Income Statement (January): Revenue $480,000 − COGS $140,000 = Gross Profit $340,000 (70.8%) − Operating Expenses $98,000 = Net Income $242,000 (50.4%).

Balance Sheet (Jan 31): Total Assets (Cash ~$336,250 + AR ~$997,700 + Prepaids + Workstations net of depreciation + others) = Total Liabilities (AP + Accrued Liabilities + Loan Payable + Deferred Revenue) + Equity (Owner's + Retained Earnings raised by $242,000 net income). Confirms Assets = Liabilities + Equity.

Cash Flow (indirect, simplified): Net Income $242,000 + Depreciation $1,000 (add back non-cash) + changes in working capital (increase in AR −$480,000 from HLD invoice, decrease in AP, etc.) + Investing (no capex in Jan) + Financing (loan interest accrual is non-cash) = reconciles ending Cash to the bank rec's $336,250. If it doesn't tie, the discrepancy is in the working-capital changes — trace each AR/AP movement.

CFO memo template: "January delivered $242,000 of net income on $480,000 of revenue, with strong 70.8% gross margins. My concern is cash — DSO is ~76 days and $112,700 of TeleCom Builders AR is overdue. In February, watch TeleCom collections and the $215,000 Southeast Broadband dispute."
